title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)
oneLiner: Cutaneous SCC is a keratinocyte carcinoma; metastases are rare but occur in high-risk lesions (size >2cm, depth >4mm, immunosuppression) – early excision cures most.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway compromise from head/neck SCC (e.g. lip, ear, scalp)** – stridor, hoarseness, tracheal deviation, or rapidly enlarging neck mass. The single finding: **stridor or respiratory distress** overlying a known lesion.
- **Haemorrhage from a large fungating SCC** – active visible bleeding, haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension). The single finding: **visible arterial spurting** or **pale/clammy patient with a weeping tumour**.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Painless, growing lump or ulcer, often on sun-exposed areas (face, scalp, ears, dorsum of hands, lower lip)
  - May be crusted, scaling, or bleeding spontaneously
  - Occasionally tender (if inflamed or perineural invasion)
  - Rapid growth (weeks) distinguishes from BCC
- Key exam findings:
  - Firm, indurated, keratotic plaque or nodule with rolled, sloping edges
  - Central ulceration with a raised, everted rim
  - ± palpable regional lymph nodes (check pre-auricular, submandibular, cervical, axillary, inguinal)
  - Look for **perineural invasion** signs: facial numbness, paraesthesia, motor weakness (especially if head/neck)
  - Satellite lesions or in-transit metastases (rare)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Basal cell carcinoma (BCC)** – slower growth, pearly rolled edge + telangiectasias, less keratotic, rarely metastases
- **Keratoacanthoma** – rapidly growing (weeks), central keratin crater, often self-resolves; can be hard to distinguish histologically
- **Actinic keratosis** – rough, dry, scaly macule, no induration or ulceration
- **Cutaneous melanoma** – pigmented, irregular borders, ≥6mm colour variation; dermoscopy helps
- **Inflamed seborrhoeic keratosis** – stuck-on appearance, greasy, no invasion
- **Pyogenic granuloma** – rapid growth, friable, bleeds easily, often with a pedicle
- **Splitter:** “BCC has pearly rolled edges and telangiectasias; SCC is keratotic, firm, and may have a central ulcer with raised, sloping margins – if you see a bleeding, indurated ulcer >1cm, think SCC first.”
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** 
  - Full skin examination (sun-exposed sites, mucosal surfaces)
  - Palpate regional lymph nodes (size, firmness, mobility)
  - Dermoscopy (if available): white circles, blood vessels in a radial pattern, scale, pink background
- **Bloods:** 
  - Not routine unless suspected metastasis or pre-op for high-risk patient
  - Consider FBC, LFTs, calcium (metastatic), and immunosuppression screen (HIV, transplant status) if recurrent/aggressive
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **None** for low-risk SCC (confirmed by excision biopsy)
  - Second-line (for high-risk SCC: size >2cm, depth >4mm, perineural invasion, recurrent, immunosuppressed): **MRI** to assess perineural spread or deep invasion; **CT** neck/chest/abdomen for nodal or distant metastases
  - **Lymph node ultrasound ± FNA** if nodes palpable
- **Special tests:**
  - **Punch or incisional biopsy** (if >2cm or uncertain diagnosis) – take from the most indurated edge; include deep dermis
  - **Histology** with margin assessment (clear margins ≥1mm for low-risk)
  - **Complete excision** with 4-6mm margins (low-risk) or wider (high-risk, ≥6mm) is both diagnostic and therapeutic
- Diagnostic criteria: **NICE high-risk SCC criteria** (any of: size >2cm, depth >4mm, Clark level ≥IV, perineural/lymphovascular invasion, recurrent, immunosuppressed, ear/lip site, poor differentiation).
- **Never-miss test:** **Biopsy of any suspicious lesion** before assuming benign; **lymph node examination** in every patient with SCC – never omit this.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** 
  - **Bleeding**: direct pressure, haemostatic agents (e.g. Surgicel, gauze packing), if severe → transfer to surgical team for ligation/ suturing
  - **Airway compromise**: immediate ENT/anaesthetic review; high-flow O₂, consider awake fibreoptic intubation if stridor severe; call senior anaesthetist
  - **Otherwise**: no emergency treatment – referral to dermatology or plastic surgery for elective excision
- **Ward (first 24-72h) – if admitted for surgery:**
  - Pre-operative assessment (ECG, bloods, if needed)
  - Mark lesion with indelible pen (or photo) – avoid shaving removal until definitive plan
  - Analgesia: simple analgesia (paracetamol 1g QDS, + NSAIDs if no contraindication – e.g. ibuprofen 400mg TDS PO)
  - If infected: swab for MCS, start flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO (or clindamycin 300mg QDS PO if allergic) – [check local policy]
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if weight >50kg) – [check local policy]
  - NBM from midnight if GA planned; IV fluids (Hartmann's 1L over 8h) if needed
  - TED stockings if immobile
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Low-risk SCC** (<2cm, depth <4mm, no high-risk features): excision with 4-6mm margins, check histology – if clear, discharge with sun protection advice and self-monitoring
  - **High-risk SCC**: wider excision (≥6mm), consider Mohs micrographic surgery for anatomical areas (face, ears) or incompletely excised margins
  - **Adjuvant radiotherapy**: if perineural invasion, incomplete resection, or nodal involvement
  - **Advanced/metastatic SCC**: immunotherapy (cemiplimab or pembrolizumab) under oncology – not for FY1 to initiate
  - **Surveillance**: high-risk patients – clinical review every 3-6 months for 2 years, then annually (NICE guidance)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** 
  - Fluids: Hartmann's 1L over 8h if NBM
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if weight >50kg, eGFR >30) – [check local policy]
  - Diet: NBM if theatre planned; restart soft diet post-surgery
  - Observations: routine 4-hourly (post-op: 1-2 hourly for 4h then 4-hourly)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day); Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (if no GI/renal/bleeding risk)
- **First-line antibiotics (if infected):** Flucloxacillin 500mg PO QDS for 7 days (IV if severe)
- **Alternative:** Clindamycin 300mg PO QDS (if penicillin allergy)
- **For advanced SCC (specialist only):** Cemiplimab 350mg IV every 3 weeks (check oncology protocol)
- **Key contraindications:** 
  - NSAIDs: avoid in active peptic ulcer, severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), anticoagulant use
  - Flucloxacillin: caution in hepatic impairment; avoid in penicillin allergy
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** 
  - Flucloxacillin: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; may cause cholestatic hepatitis – stop if jaundice
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose in mild-moderate renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing high-dose steroids to an infected SCC** – can worsen infection and mask perineural spread. **Over-prescribing NSAIDs in elderly** – acute kidney injury and GI bleed risk.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** 
  - Haemorrhage (especially large fungating lesions)
  - Wound infection/dehiscence after excision
  - Nerve damage (facial nerve branch injury from head/neck surgery)
- **Chronic:**
  - Local recurrence (especially if margins involved, high-risk features)
  - Regional nodal metastasis (most common – parotid, cervical, axillary)
  - Distant metastasis (lung, liver, bone – rare)
  - Disfigurement
sections.Prognosis: - Low-risk SCC: >95% cure rate with adequate local excision. High-risk SCC: 10-20% risk of nodal metastasis; 5-year survival for metastatic SCC ~30-50%. Immunocompromised patients have higher recurrence and metastasis rates.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Always measure the lesion** – document size (longest axis), depth (if known), and body site. Use a ruler.
2. **Palpate regional lymph nodes** in every patient – if palpable, arrange ultrasound/FNA before planning surgery.
3. **Ask about immunosuppression** – transplant recipients, HIV, long-term steroids, anti-TNF therapy – these require urgent referral and wider margins.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Don't assume a small lesion is low-risk** – check histology for depth and aggressive features.
2. **Don't delay biopsy** – if you see a suspicious indurated ulcer, do a punch biopsy or refer within 2 weeks.
3. **Don't neglect lymph node examination** – missing a regional metastasis is a serious omission.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - Wound becomes red, hot, purulent or breaks down after excision
  - New bleeding or discharge from the lesion
  - New lump near the scar or in the neck/groin/axilla
  - Unexplained weight loss, fever, night sweats
- **Follow-up:** 
  - Biopsy or excision results: review in 2 weeks
  - High-risk SCC: dermatology/plastic surgery follow-up every 3-6 months for 2 years
  - Low-risk: discharge with advice to self-monitor and report new lesions
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:** 
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L (e.g. from haemorrhage or airway compromise)
  - GCS <15 or stridor
  - **Explicit thresholds:** 
    - Respiratory rate >25 or <8
    - SpO₂ <90% on 15L O₂
    - Systolic BP <90 mmHg
    - Heart rate >130 bpm
  - Any signs of airway compromise (stridor, hoarseness, tracheal tug) – urgent ENT/anaesthetics review
  - Rapid lesion growth (>30% in 2 weeks) – consider urgent imaging for perineural invasion

rawMarkdown:
## Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)
**One-liner:** Cutaneous SCC is a keratinocyte carcinoma; metastases are rare but occur in high-risk lesions (size >2cm, depth >4mm, immunosuppression) – early excision cures most.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway compromise from head/neck SCC (e.g. lip, ear, scalp)** – stridor, hoarseness, tracheal deviation, or rapidly enlarging neck mass. The single finding: **stridor or respiratory distress** overlying a known lesion.
- **Haemorrhage from a large fungating SCC** – active visible bleeding, haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension). The single finding: **visible arterial spurting** or **pale/clammy patient with a weeping tumour**.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Painless, growing lump or ulcer, often on sun-exposed areas (face, scalp, ears, dorsum of hands, lower lip)
  - May be crusted, scaling, or bleeding spontaneously
  - Occasionally tender (if inflamed or perineural invasion)
  - Rapid growth (weeks) distinguishes from BCC
- Key exam findings:
  - Firm, indurated, keratotic plaque or nodule with rolled, sloping edges
  - Central ulceration with a raised, everted rim
  - ± palpable regional lymph nodes (check pre-auricular, submandibular, cervical, axillary, inguinal)
  - Look for **perineural invasion** signs: facial numbness, paraesthesia, motor weakness (especially if head/neck)
  - Satellite lesions or in-transit metastases (rare)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Basal cell carcinoma (BCC)** – slower growth, pearly rolled edge + telangiectasias, less keratotic, rarely metastases
- **Keratoacanthoma** – rapidly growing (weeks), central keratin crater, often self-resolves; can be hard to distinguish histologically
- **Actinic keratosis** – rough, dry, scaly macule, no induration or ulceration
- **Cutaneous melanoma** – pigmented, irregular borders, ≥6mm colour variation; dermoscopy helps
- **Inflamed seborrhoeic keratosis** – stuck-on appearance, greasy, no invasion
- **Pyogenic granuloma** – rapid growth, friable, bleeds easily, often with a pedicle
- **Splitter:** “BCC has pearly rolled edges and telangiectasias; SCC is keratotic, firm, and may have a central ulcer with raised, sloping margins – if you see a bleeding, indurated ulcer >1cm, think SCC first.”

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** 
  - Full skin examination (sun-exposed sites, mucosal surfaces)
  - Palpate regional lymph nodes (size, firmness, mobility)
  - Dermoscopy (if available): white circles, blood vessels in a radial pattern, scale, pink background
- **Bloods:** 
  - Not routine unless suspected metastasis or pre-op for high-risk patient
  - Consider FBC, LFTs, calcium (metastatic), and immunosuppression screen (HIV, transplant status) if recurrent/aggressive
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **None** for low-risk SCC (confirmed by excision biopsy)
  - Second-line (for high-risk SCC: size >2cm, depth >4mm, perineural invasion, recurrent, immunosuppressed): **MRI** to assess perineural spread or deep invasion; **CT** neck/chest/abdomen for nodal or distant metastases
  - **Lymph node ultrasound ± FNA** if nodes palpable
- **Special tests:**
  - **Punch or incisional biopsy** (if >2cm or uncertain diagnosis) – take from the most indurated edge; include deep dermis
  - **Histology** with margin assessment (clear margins ≥1mm for low-risk)
  - **Complete excision** with 4-6mm margins (low-risk) or wider (high-risk, ≥6mm) is both diagnostic and therapeutic
- Diagnostic criteria: **NICE high-risk SCC criteria** (any of: size >2cm, depth >4mm, Clark level ≥IV, perineural/lymphovascular invasion, recurrent, immunosuppressed, ear/lip site, poor differentiation).
- **Never-miss test:** **Biopsy of any suspicious lesion** before assuming benign; **lymph node examination** in every patient with SCC – never omit this.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** 
  - **Bleeding**: direct pressure, haemostatic agents (e.g. Surgicel, gauze packing), if severe → transfer to surgical team for ligation/ suturing
  - **Airway compromise**: immediate ENT/anaesthetic review; high-flow O₂, consider awake fibreoptic intubation if stridor severe; call senior anaesthetist
  - **Otherwise**: no emergency treatment – referral to dermatology or plastic surgery for elective excision
- **Ward (first 24-72h) – if admitted for surgery:**
  - Pre-operative assessment (ECG, bloods, if needed)
  - Mark lesion with indelible pen (or photo) – avoid shaving removal until definitive plan
  - Analgesia: simple analgesia (paracetamol 1g QDS, + NSAIDs if no contraindication – e.g. ibuprofen 400mg TDS PO)
  - If infected: swab for MCS, start flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO (or clindamycin 300mg QDS PO if allergic) – [check local policy]
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if weight >50kg) – [check local policy]
  - NBM from midnight if GA planned; IV fluids (Hartmann's 1L over 8h) if needed
  - TED stockings if immobile
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Low-risk SCC** (<2cm, depth <4mm, no high-risk features): excision with 4-6mm margins, check histology – if clear, discharge with sun protection advice and self-monitoring
  - **High-risk SCC**: wider excision (≥6mm), consider Mohs micrographic surgery for anatomical areas (face, ears) or incompletely excised margins
  - **Adjuvant radiotherapy**: if perineural invasion, incomplete resection, or nodal involvement
  - **Advanced/metastatic SCC**: immunotherapy (cemiplimab or pembrolizumab) under oncology – not for FY1 to initiate
  - **Surveillance**: high-risk patients – clinical review every 3-6 months for 2 years, then annually (NICE guidance)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** 
  - Fluids: Hartmann's 1L over 8h if NBM
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if weight >50kg, eGFR >30) – [check local policy]
  - Diet: NBM if theatre planned; restart soft diet post-surgery
  - Observations: routine 4-hourly (post-op: 1-2 hourly for 4h then 4-hourly)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day); Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS (if no GI/renal/bleeding risk)
- **First-line antibiotics (if infected):** Flucloxacillin 500mg PO QDS for 7 days (IV if severe)
- **Alternative:** Clindamycin 300mg PO QDS (if penicillin allergy)
- **For advanced SCC (specialist only):** Cemiplimab 350mg IV every 3 weeks (check oncology protocol)
- **Key contraindications:** 
  - NSAIDs: avoid in active peptic ulcer, severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), anticoagulant use
  - Flucloxacillin: caution in hepatic impairment; avoid in penicillin allergy
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** 
  - Flucloxacillin: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; may cause cholestatic hepatitis – stop if jaundice
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; reduce dose in mild-moderate renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing high-dose steroids to an infected SCC** – can worsen infection and mask perineural spread. **Over-prescribing NSAIDs in elderly** – acute kidney injury and GI bleed risk.

### Complications
- **Acute:** 
  - Haemorrhage (especially large fungating lesions)
  - Wound infection/dehiscence after excision
  - Nerve damage (facial nerve branch injury from head/neck surgery)
- **Chronic:**
  - Local recurrence (especially if margins involved, high-risk features)
  - Regional nodal metastasis (most common – parotid, cervical, axillary)
  - Distant metastasis (lung, liver, bone – rare)
  - Disfigurement

### Prognosis
- Low-risk SCC: >95% cure rate with adequate local excision. High-risk SCC: 10-20% risk of nodal metastasis; 5-year survival for metastatic SCC ~30-50%. Immunocompromised patients have higher recurrence and metastasis rates.

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Always measure the lesion** – document size (longest axis), depth (if known), and body site. Use a ruler.
2. **Palpate regional lymph nodes** in every patient – if palpable, arrange ultrasound/FNA before planning surgery.
3. **Ask about immunosuppression** – transplant recipients, HIV, long-term steroids, anti-TNF therapy – these require urgent referral and wider margins.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Don't assume a small lesion is low-risk** – check histology for depth and aggressive features.
2. **Don't delay biopsy** – if you see a suspicious indurated ulcer, do a punch biopsy or refer within 2 weeks.
3. **Don't neglect lymph node examination** – missing a regional metastasis is a serious omission.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - Wound becomes red, hot, purulent or breaks down after excision
  - New bleeding or discharge from the lesion
  - New lump near the scar or in the neck/groin/axilla
  - Unexplained weight loss, fever, night sweats
- **Follow-up:** 
  - Biopsy or excision results: review in 2 weeks
  - High-risk SCC: dermatology/plastic surgery follow-up every 3-6 months for 2 years
  - Low-risk: discharge with advice to self-monitor and report new lesions

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:** 
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L (e.g. from haemorrhage or airway compromise)
  - GCS <15 or stridor
  - **Explicit thresholds:** 
    - Respiratory rate >25 or <8
    - SpO₂ <90% on 15L O₂
    - Systolic BP <90 mmHg
    - Heart rate >130 bpm
  - Any signs of airway compromise (stridor, hoarseness, tracheal tug) – urgent ENT/anaesthetics review
  - Rapid lesion growth (>30% in 2 weeks) – consider urgent imaging for perineural invasion
